title: Pork pie
subtitle: Hand-raised hot water crust filled with seasoned pork and finished with jelly
excerpt: One large pork pie raised in a 900 g loaf tin: a hot water crust pastry worked while warm and malleable, filled with seasoned pork shoulder and belly, baked until deep amber, then filled with a pork stock jelly through the steam vent after cooling. Slices cleanly when cold.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the raised pork pie chapter documents hot water crust made by melting lard in boiling water, then beating into flour while hot to produce a workable paste, which is moulded by hand around a wooden dolly or pressed into a tin while still warm. The chapter documents the pork and herb filling and the practice of filling the cooled pie with a jellied stock through a hole in the lid. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's raised pie section documents the same method for hot water crust and the filling of cold pork pie with jellied broth. The recipe here follows the public-domain method.

content.text: Hot water crust pastry behaves in the opposite way to shortcrust. Where shortcrust requires cold fat and cold hands to stay short,
must be worked while warm. Once cold it becomes rigid and cracks. The window for moulding is about 15 minutes. Work quickly and have the filling ready before you start the pastry.

items.name: Lard
items.prepNote: or 60 g lard + 60 g unsalted butter

items.name: Pork shoulder
items.prepNote: minced or very finely chopped by hand

items.name: Pork belly
items.prepNote: minced or very finely chopped, the fat keeps the filling moist

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: the filling needs strong seasoning to carry through the pastry

items.name: Chicken stock
items.prepNote: good quality, the jelly flavour comes entirely from the stock

content.text: Mix the {{pork-shoulder}} pork shoulder, {{pork-belly}} belly, {{sage-dried}}, {{nutmeg-ground}}, {{black-pepper-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a bowl until combined. Fry a small piece in a dry pan and taste for seasoning, the filling must be well seasoned as it cools to room temperature when served and the pastry is thick. Refrigerate while you make the pastry.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease a 900 g loaf tin. Put the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} salt in a large bowl and make a well. Bring the {{lard}} and {{water}} to a full boil in a small saucepan, the lard must be fully melted. Pour immediately into the flour and beat with a wooden spoon until a smooth, soft dough forms. It will be warm and pliable. Work quickly: the dough is mouldable for about 15 minutes before it stiffens.

Reserve one quarter of the dough for the lid. Press the remaining dough into the tin while still warm, using your knuckles to push it up the sides to an even 5 to 6 mm thickness. Work up the sides before the base, the weight of the filling will compact the base further. Pack the cold filling tightly into the case, doming it slightly above the pastry sides (it will shrink during baking). Roll the reserved dough into a lid slightly larger than the tin. Press it on, crimp the edges together firmly with a fork. Cut a 2 cm steam vent in the centre. Brush with {{eggs}} egg wash.
Bake, 20 minutes at 200°C then 60 to 70 minutes at 160°C

Bake at 200°C fan for 20 minutes until the pastry begins to set. Reduce to 160°C fan and bake for a further 60 to 70 minutes until deep amber all over and a meat thermometer pushed through the steam vent reads at least 75°C in the centre of the filling. Brush with egg wash again after 30 minutes for a deep shine. Rest in the tin for 30 minutes, then remove and cool completely on a rack, at least 2 hours.

Soak the {{gelatine-leaves}} gelatine leaves in cold water for 5 minutes until soft. Warm the {{chicken-stock}} stock until barely simmering. Squeeze out the gelatine and stir into the warm stock until dissolved. Leave to cool until the jelly is at room temperature but still liquid. Using a small funnel or a piping bag, pour the jelly through the steam vent in small amounts, pausing between each to allow it to settle into the gaps in the filling. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight. Slice cold.
